challenge_summary: The plan's core assumption — that duplicated content can be replaced with cross-file pointers — conflicts with VS Code's instruction auto-injection architecture. Phase 1 would dismantle an intentional safety net documented in the code itself, and ignores a third sync source (validate-artifact-templates.mjs ARTIFACT_HEADINGS). Phase 6 renames create broken intermediate commits before Phase 8 fixes references. Tag consolidation scope is underestimated (15 locations, not 3). The cost-estimate instruction file provides detailed per-section guidance beyond bare H2 headings, and stripping it would lose content that has no equivalent elsewhere. Several phases assume agents will reliably follow 'go read file X' pointers in auto-injected instructions, which is a fundamentally different mechanism from having the content present.

issues.title: Phase 1 dismantles intentional dual-loading safety net documented in the codebase
issues.description: azure-artifacts.instructions.md lines 7-13 explicitly state: 'This instruction file and the azure-artifacts skill (SKILL.md) intentionally contain the same H2 heading lists. The SKILL.md is the authoritative source; this instruction file is the enforcement trigger via applyTo scope.' The duplication is by design. Instructions auto-inject via applyTo when agents generate artifact output files — this is passive, guaranteed, and zero-hop. Skills require agents to actively execute 'Read SKILL.md' directives — this is active, agent-dependent, and one-hop. Replacing H2 content with a pointer turns a guaranteed delivery mechanism into a two-step process that depends on the agent following the pointer instruction. If the agent skips or deprioritizes the pointer (e.g., context window pressure, model behavior variance), it will generate artifacts without H2 heading guidance, producing non-compliant outputs that fail validation.
issues.failure_scenario: An agent generating 06-deployment-summary.md gets the slimmed instruction file auto-injected (just a pointer to SKILL.md), but due to context window saturation or model behavior, doesn't follow the 'Read SKILL.md' instruction. It produces artifacts with invented H2 headings. The validate-artifact-templates.mjs pre-commit hook catches the error, but the developer must re-run the agent — a silent regression in agent reliability.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Keep the H2 heading lists in the instruction file. Instead, deduplicate the prose/rules surrounding them. The instruction file should remain the 'enforcement trigger' with actual heading content; reduce only the non-heading explanatory text that duplicates SKILL.md. This preserves the zero-hop auto-injection guarantee while still reducing LOC by ~200 lines.

issues.title: Phase 1 ignores the third sync source: ARTIFACT_HEADINGS in validate-artifact-templates.mjs
issues.description: validate-h2-sync.mjs (lines 1-12) explicitly validates THREE sources in sync: (1) SKILL.md, (2) azure-artifacts.instructions.md, (3) ARTIFACT_HEADINGS in validate-artifact-templates.mjs. The plan only discusses two sources. If Phase 1 removes H2 headings from the instruction file, the sync validator would error on every artifact type with 'missing from azure-artifacts'. The plan says 'Update validation to check SKILL.md as the single canonical source' but this means rewriting the entire tri-source sync architecture, not just 'adjusting logic'. The validate-artifact-templates.mjs ARTIFACT_HEADINGS would become orphaned with no sync partner.
issues.failure_scenario: After Phase 1, running 'npm run validate:h2-sync' immediately fails with 15 errors ('missing from azure-artifacts' for every artifact type). The developer realizes the sync validator needs a fundamental rewrite, not a minor adjustment, expanding the Phase 1 scope significantly.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Acknowledge the three-source sync architecture explicitly in the plan. If the goal is a single source of truth, the plan must also address what happens to ARTIFACT_HEADINGS in validate-artifact-templates.mjs and whether the sync validator should be replaced entirely or restructured to validate SKILL.md against the validator only.

issues.title: Phase 6 renames + Phase 8 reference fixes create broken intermediate commits
issues.description: The commit strategy specifies Phase 6 as a standalone commit ('refactor(instructions): rename files for clarity') and Phase 8 as a separate commit ('fix(refs): update all downstream references'). Between these commits, every reference to the old filenames (agents-definitions.instructions.md in lefthook.yml line 100, docs-writer SKILL.md line 52, repo-architecture.md line 101; self-explanatory-code-commenting in multiple system prompt instruction blocks; update-docs-on-code-change) is broken. CI validation would fail on the Phase 6 commit. This violates the plan's own success metric of 'npm run validate:all passes' per phase.
issues.failure_scenario: Phase 6 commit renames agents-definitions.instructions.md to agent-definitions.instructions.md. lefthook.yml line 100 still references the old name. The pre-commit hook itself references a non-existent file in its fail_text message. CI fails. The branch is in a broken state until Phase 8 lands.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Merge Phase 6 and Phase 8 into a single atomic commit per rename. For each file rename, update ALL references in the same commit. Alternatively, restructure Phase 6 to include reference updates inline, and remove Phase 8 as a separate phase.

issues.title: Cross-file pointer approach ('Read X for details') is non-deterministic in auto-injected instructions
issues.description: The plan's core consolidation strategy (Phases 1, 2, 3) relies on instruction files saying 'Read SKILL.md for the actual content.' But instruction files are auto-injected by VS Code based on applyTo patterns when matching files are being edited. The agent receives the instruction content passively. A pointer instruction ('go read another file') requires the agent to actively perform a file-read action. This is a fundamentally different mechanism. There is no VS Code feature that auto-loads referenced files from within instruction content. The agent must interpret the pointer and issue a tool call, which may not happen if: (a) the agent's context window is saturated, (b) the model deprioritizes it, (c) the agent is run in a mode without file-read tools, or (d) the pointer text is not compelling enough for the model to act on.
issues.failure_scenario: The workload-documentation.instructions.md already contains a pointer to SKILL.md for styling ('📚 [Azure Artifacts Skill](../skills/azure-artifacts/SKILL.md)') at line 14, yet it ALSO includes inline styling rules. This suggests the repository maintainers already discovered that pointers alone are insufficient and added inline content as a safety net. Removing the inline content would regress to the pointer-only approach that was apparently already deemed inadequate.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Validate the pointer approach empirically before committing to it as the consolidation strategy. Run a controlled test: strip H2 headings from a copy of the instruction file, add only a pointer, and test whether 3 different agents (03-Architect, 06-Bicep Code Generator, 08-As-Built) reliably follow the pointer to produce correct artifacts. If they don't, the entire consolidation strategy needs rethinking.

issues.title: Phase 3 tag consolidation scope is 5x larger than stated (15 locations, not 3)
issues.description: The plan says tag rules ('Environment, ManagedBy, Project, Owner') are repeated in '3 Bicep/governance files' and proposes consolidating to 1. But grep reveals 15 locations referencing these tags: copilot-instructions.md, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, ISSUE_TEMPLATE/copilot-agent-task.yml, governance-discovery.instructions.md (as an example of what could go wrong), bicep-code-best-practices.instructions.md, 02-requirements.agent.md, 05-bicep-planner.agent.md, 06-bicep-code-generator.agent.md, docs/GLOSSARY.md, docs/troubleshooting.md, plan-bicep.prompt.md, generate-bicep.prompt.md, and agent output artifacts. Consolidating only 3 instruction files leaves 12+ stale references that could contradict or confuse agents.
issues.failure_scenario: After Phase 3, the tag list in bicep-code-best-practices.instructions.md is the 'canonical source', but 06-bicep-code-generator.agent.md line 172 still says 'Apply all 4 required tags (Environment, ManagedBy, Project, Owner)' directly in its body. An agent following its own body text would use hardcoded tags instead of reading the canonical source, defeating the consolidation.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Expand Phase 3 scope to audit and update ALL 15 tag reference locations. For agent.md files that embed tag lists, replace with 'Read azure-defaults SKILL.md for required tags' (which agents already do per their skill-loading directives). For templates/docs, add a note that the list is illustrative and the canonical source is bicep-code-best-practices.

issues.title: Phase 6 rename reference scope doesn't include lefthook.yml, docs-writer skill, or repo-architecture reference
issues.description: The plan lists 6 locations to check for rename references (copilot-instructions.md, agents, skills, scripts, workflows, docs). But actual grep shows agents-definitions.instructions.md is also referenced in: lefthook.yml line 100 (pre-commit hook fail_text), .github/skills/docs-writer/SKILL.md line 52, and .github/skills/docs-writer/references/repo-architecture.md line 101. These are not in the scan scope for Phase 6 or Phase 8.
issues.failure_scenario: After renaming to agent-definitions.instructions.md, the lefthook pre-commit hook displays an error message pointing developers to a non-existent file for reference. The docs-writer skill has stale cross-references that could cause the docs-writer agent to reference a missing file.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Add lefthook.yml, CHANGELOG.md, .github/skills/*/references/*.md to the reference scan scope. Use the proposed Phase 7 validation script to catch these before committing, which means Phase 7 should execute BEFORE Phase 6.

issues.title: Phase 1 line estimate (510 → ~80) is likely unrealistic; actual non-H2 content is ~160 lines
issues.description: azure-artifacts.instructions.md is 510 lines. The H2 heading blocks account for roughly 350 lines. The remaining ~160 lines contain: frontmatter (8), pre-generation workflow (8), template mapping table (20), golden rule (2), standard components table (10), enforcement layers (10), quick fix command section (12), common errors section (15), generation workflow (8), why this matters section (8), quick reference card (20), and various section headers and spacing (~40). Getting to ~80 lines means cutting half of this operational guidance, not just removing H2 headings.
issues.failure_scenario: The implementer removes H2 headings and arrives at ~160 lines, then must decide which operational guidance to also remove to hit the ~80 target. Template mapping, enforcement layers, and quick fix commands are unique to this file and have no equivalent in SKILL.md. Removing them creates an operational guidance gap.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Revise the target to ~180 lines (removal of H2 heading blocks only, preserving all operational guidance). Or explicitly list which non-H2 sections to cut and verify they have equivalents elsewhere.

issues.title: No rollback strategy if agent output quality regresses
issues.description: The plan's success metrics include 'Agent output quality: No regression' but defines only manual spot-checks as verification. There is no baseline capture of current agent output quality, no automated regression test, and no rollback plan if agents produce degraded output after the changes. The 'spot-check' approach is subjective and non-reproducible.
issues.failure_scenario: After Phase 1-3 land, agents produce artifacts with subtly wrong formatting (e.g., missing emoji prefixes on H2 headings, wrong callout styles). This is only discovered weeks later during an actual project engagement. The changes are already merged and it's unclear which phase introduced the regression.
issues.artifact_section: Phase 9 — Manual Spot Checks / Success Metrics
issues.suggested_mitigation: Add a baseline capture step in Phase 0: run each agent on a test project and save the outputs. After Phase 9, re-run the same agents and diff the outputs. If H2 headings or styling differ, the regression is caught. Alternatively, rely on the existing validate-artifact-templates.mjs against the saved baseline outputs.

issues.title: Phase 2 creates a sub-reference file requiring multi-hop loading that has no precedent in this repo
issues.description: The plan proposes creating .github/skills/azure-artifacts/references/styling-standards.md and having SKILL.md say 'Read references/styling-standards.md'. Currently, the azure-artifacts skill has no references/ directory (only templates/). This introduces a new loading pattern: SKILL.md → references file. Agents would need to: (1) be invoked, (2) read SKILL.md via skill loading, (3) follow a pointer to read references/styling-standards.md via a third file read. This triple-hop loading has no precedent in the repo and relies on agents performing sequential file reads reliably.
issues.failure_scenario: The 08-As-Built agent reads SKILL.md, sees 'Read references/styling-standards.md', but deprioritizes it because the styling section was already summarized in the SKILL.md pointer text. It generates documentation without the full styling rules, producing inconsistent callout usage.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Keep styling rules inline in SKILL.md (which is the skill's single file that agents are guaranteed to read). Instead, remove the duplicated styling rules from markdown.instructions.md and workload-documentation.instructions.md only, making SKILL.md the single source. This achieves deduplication without introducing a sub-reference hop.

issues.title: Phase 4 code-review.instructions.md applyTo trimming may conflict with system-level instruction registration
issues.description: The system prompt's <instructions> block lists code-review.instructions.md with applyTo: '**/*.{js,mjs,cjs,ts,tsx,jsx,py,ps1,sh,bicep,tf}'. This appears to be dynamically read from the file's frontmatter. Trimming the applyTo in the instruction file will also change which files trigger the instruction. The plan says to 'remove non-existent file types (.ts, .tsx, .jsx, .tf)' but these types might be intentionally included for future-proofing. Additionally, .tf (Terraform) appears in the repo's terraform-roadmap.md docs — Terraform support may be planned.
issues.failure_scenario: A developer adds a .ts validation script to the repo in 3 months. The code-review instruction doesn't apply to it because .ts was removed from the applyTo pattern. The developer doesn't realize code review standards exist because they never get auto-injected.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Only remove file patterns that are explicitly ruled out of the project's roadmap. Keep .tf if Terraform support is planned per docs/terraform-roadmap.md. For .ts/.tsx/.jsx, verify they are truly never planned before removal.

issues.title: Phase 5 '_archived' directory may trigger instruction auto-scanning
issues.description: Moving copilot-thought-logging.instructions.md to .github/instructions/_archived/ preserves the file in the .github/instructions/ directory tree. VS Code may still discover files matching *.instructions.md in subdirectories for auto-loading. If the VS Code instruction scanner is recursive, the archived file could still be loaded.
issues.failure_scenario: The archived instruction file continues to appear in VS Code's instruction discovery, causing confusion about whether it's active. Developers see it listed and assume it's in use.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Delete the file outright instead of archiving (git history preserves it). If archiving is preferred, use a location outside .github/instructions/ such as .github/_archived/ to avoid instruction scanner discovery.

issues.title: Phase 7 validation script execution ordering creates a chicken-and-egg problem
issues.description: Phase 7 creates validate-instruction-references.mjs which catches broken references. But Phases 1-6 make changes that could introduce broken references. The validation script doesn't exist during Phases 1-6 execution, so broken references introduced in those phases won't be caught until Phase 7. By then, the developer has committed 6 phases of changes.
issues.failure_scenario: Phase 3 adds a cross-reference from governance-discovery.instructions.md to bicep-code-best-practices.instructions.md. The reference path has a typo. This isn't caught until Phase 7 when the validation script is created, requiring a fixup commit that retroactively fixes Phase 3 output.
issues.suggested_mitigation: Move Phase 7 (create validation script) to Phase 0.5, immediately after the baseline capture. Run it as a pre-check after each subsequent phase to catch reference issues as they're introduced.

issues.title: cost-estimate.instructions.md provides unique per-section content guidance, not just H2 headings
issues.description: The plan says cost-estimate.instructions.md has 'duplicated H2 headings' to remove. But the file (430 lines) contains detailed per-section guidance: what to include under 'Cost At-a-Glance', formatting templates for budget utilization bars, decision summary bullet patterns, visual standards (status indicators, category icons, trend indicators), MCP tool requirements, and a 'Base Run Cost vs Growth-Variable Cost' section that doesn't exist in SKILL.md or the artifact template. Treating this as simple H2 duplication understates the unique content.
issues.failure_scenario: After slimming cost-estimate.instructions.md by 30%, the detailed per-section formatting guidance (e.g., 'include a confidence line', 'use budget utilization bar format') is lost. The 03-Architect and 08-As-Built agents produce cost estimates that are structurally correct (right H2 headings) but poorly formatted (missing utilization bars, no confidence indicators).
issues.suggested_mitigation: Audit cost-estimate.instructions.md to separate true H2 duplication (the bare heading list in 'Core Heading Contract') from unique per-section guidance. Only remove the bare heading list (~20 lines). Preserve the per-section formatting templates and visual standards (~350 lines of unique content).
